description: 'laniru' gathers travel across a span of distance or time into one extended event. The route may be planned or improvised, and whether the traveller changes is left to the story. 'wepu' (go) can cover any trip, while 'whenola' (wander) foregrounds the absence of a fixed route or destination.
articulatory_notes: Place the tongue tip at the alveolar ridge for voiced lateral 'l', let the voice pass around its sides, and open into central 'a'. Touch the tongue tip at the backs of the upper teeth for dental 'n' and carry nasal voice into stressed high-front 'i'. Tap or briefly trill the tongue at the alveolar ridge for 'r', then round the lips and raise the back of the tongue for high-back 'u'.
sound_symbolism: Three syllables keep the tongue travelling through lateral, nasal, and tapped contacts. Beside compact 'wepu' (go), the longer form gives an extended passage more room in the mouth.
usage_notes: A source takes 'lue' (ABL), an open direction takes 'wea' (TOWARD), and a reached destination takes 'kau' (ALL). As an event noun, 'laniru' can modify 'miona' (person) to describe a traveller.

examples.phi: lo miona lue sileta thorui to laniru.
examples.translation: The people journeyed from the east.

semantic_domains.activity: extended travel gathered into one event
semantic_domains.spatial: a passage across a substantial route
semantic_domains.temporal: travel unfolding over an appreciable interval
